text: Medicinal plants constitute an important proportion of dietary substances being investigated for their potential application in alternate medicine. Thus, the objective of this work was to assess the polyphenolic constituents, in-vitro antioxidant activity, cytotoxicity along with antimicrobial efficacy of some indigenous plant extracts evaluated through various bioassays. Extracts of eight medicinal plants—Mentha longifolia (M. L) and Mentha arvensis (M. A), Tinospora cordifolia (T. C), Cymbopogon citratus (C. C), Foeniculum vulgare (F. V), Cassia absus (C. A) and Camellia sinensis (C. S), Trachyspermum ammi (T. A)—were analyzed for the evaluation of the efficacy of plant extracts. Free radical scavenging assays such as DPPH, superoxide scavenging, hydroxyl scavenging assays, nitric oxide scavenging assay were done in order to check the in-vitro antioxidant potential of the MPPE. Also enzyme inhibition tests like amylase inhibition and protein phosphatase inhibition were carried out in order to study the possible anti-diabetic properties of medicinal plant extracts. Along with this, the cytotoxic effect of MPPE on Caco-2 cell lines and antibacterial effects on certain microbial strains were assessed through MTT assay and disk diffusion method, respectively. MPPE revealed a strong free radical scavenging activity (at all concentrations tested) along with total phenolic content and total flavonoid content (TFC) values and was highly correlated with each other (r = 0.9899, p = 0.002). Also, 25 compounds representing phenolic acids, flavonoids and alkaloids were detected using UHPLC-Q-Orbitrap-Fourier Transform Mass Spectroscopy (FT-MS) in the selected species with 3-feroylquinic acid as the dominant compound (1302 mg/L). This study reveals a positive correlation of MPPE antioxidants activity with respect to their TPC and TFC content. Species-wise identification of major bioactive compound such as *M.M. longifolia*, *C. absus* and *C. S.* having rosmarinic acid (560 mg/L), chlorogenic acid (780 mg/L) and rutin (720 mg/L), respectively. However, *C. S.* exhibits highest phenolic density (34.44 × 10(3) mg/100g) by having 20, 59 and 116 % increased phenolic density values when compared with other five medicinal plant extracts. *T. cordifolia* was having an unique characteristic by exhibiting highest density of alkaloids (14.31 × 10(3) mg/100g) when compared with other species. *T. ammi* and *C. citratus* exhibited enhanced cytotoxic effect against Caco-2 cells lines whereas *M. arvensis* and *F. vulgare* exhibited pronounced enzyme inhibiting properties. In addition, *F. vulgare* and *C. S.* demonstrated effective antibacterial effects against S. aureus (25 mm) and E. coli (18 mm) respectively. Using PCA, a separation of herbal plants was clearly seen based on their unique and desired activity. Thus this study validated various applications of native plant species due to their large number of phytogenic compounds and enhances their application towards formulation of numerous pharmacological drugs.
